channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Chef René Redzepi resigns from Noma amid abuse allegations by former staff
item.description: The acclaimed chef leaves the helm of the Copenhagen restaurant as its exclusive Los Angeles pop-up begins service.

item.title: Lebanon appeals to Israel’s allies to intervene as hundreds are reported killed
item.description: As authorities in Lebanon say more than 680 people have been killed in the country in the past week, its government has appealed to U.S. and European leaders to intervene even offering to engage in once-taboo talks with Israel. Israel rejected the proposal, according to two people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Bill Barr: Federal judges rely on this guide. It just took a sharp left turn.
item.description: The judiciary needs unbiased scientific evidence, not climate advocacy.

item.title: Rep. James Clyburn, 85, to seek reelection as Democratic Party's old guard faces challenges from younger members
item.description: The decision by Clyburn, who has served more than 30 years in the House representing South Carolina, follows retirement announcements from Reps. Nancy Pelosi (D-California) and Steny H. Hoyer (D-Maryland), the other two-thirds of a leadership trio that guided House Democrats from 2007 to 2023.

item.title: Last year’s big winner in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Thursday points.

item.title: We asked 1,000 Americans if U.S. strikes on Iran should continue. Here’s what they said.
item.description: Resistance to the U.S. military campaign in Iran is easing overall, though a plurality of Americans still favor ending the strikes.

item.title: Iran poll. Tariffs. Hegseth photos. Jan. 6 plaque. AI election spending. Weight-loss drugs. Bigger breakfast. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Analysis: It’s already too late for Timothée Chalamet to win an Oscar
item.description: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences often misses the moment on best actors, leaving nominees lingering on the wait list for years.

item.title: Iranian elementary school was on U.S. target list and may have been mistaken as a military site, people familiar with strike say
item.description: The strike killed at least 175, many of them children, and has raised questions as to whether the military’s use of AI to identify targets was a factor.

item.title: D.C. cherry trees reach first stage on the way to peak bloom: Green buds
item.description: The bloom process started later than usual after colder-than-average weather from December through February. With cooler conditions expected during the middle part of March this year, the bloom process will probably unfold more slowly, with peak bloom expected between the end of March and the first week of April.

item.title: RFK Jr.’s advisers had a plan to target covid shots. Then it fell apart.
item.description: Some members of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s vaccine advisory panel have publicly questioned the safety and manufacturing of the shots, including raising a debunked theory that DNA contaminants in the vaccines are harmful. One option under consideration was a potential vote to withdraw the federal recommendation for covid-19 mRNA vaccines altogether because of those objections.

item.title: Democrats ask what happened to millions of dollars earmarked for Trump’s presidential library
item.description: ABC, Meta, Paramount and X reportedly agreed to commit at least $63 million toward the project in legal settlements with Donald Trump. The original fund was dissolved last year.

item.title: Carolyn Hax readers respond: Volatile ex-friend is back in my orbit. What do I do?
item.description: Carolyn Hax readers respond: Volatile ex-friend is back in my orbit. What do I do?

item.title: Pentagon bars press photographers over ‘unflattering’ images of Hegseth
item.description: The defense secretary’s staff took issue with images taken in a rare briefing last week and decided to shut out photographers from two subsequent news conferences, according to people familiar with the decision.

item.title: Are servers giving me fake compliments? Ask your dining questions.
item.description: Are servers giving me fake compliments? Ask your dining questions.

item.title: Nations agree to release oil reserves as war in Iran hits global economy
item.description: The International Energy Agency announced that it would carry out its largest-ever release of oil reserves — 400 million barrels — in a bid to control spiking energy prices caused by the United States and Israel’s war against Iran.

item.title: Even now, America can’t rally. What has Trump done?
item.description: The country is at war. It’s the 250th. America is still in no mood.

item.title: Government appeals judge’s order barring search of Washington Post reporter’s devices
item.description: Federal prosecutors said that the judge’s order for the court to conduct the search violates the Constitution’s separation of powers and that journalists are not protected from searches when the government fears they could possess sensitive government materials.

item.title: This city is the topic of today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.

item.title: Global Entry reopens, but airports still warn of potential long wait times
item.description: The Department of Homeland Security reinstated the program on Wednesday morning after suspending it in late February, citing the partial government shutdown.

item.title: February inflation was unchanged but predates surge in energy prices from Iran war
item.description: Gasoline, airline and shipping costs are climbing, testing the Federal Reserve and Trump administration.

item.title: Russians say Iran attack shows U.S. can’t be trusted in Ukraine talks
item.description: As Washington focuses on Iran, delaying talks on Russia’s war in Ukraine, some in Moscow say the Kremlin must achieve its goals militarily.

item.title: Drone strike. Election takeaways. Trump library. DOGE data. Covid shots. School vouchers. Running tips. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Drone hits major U.S. diplomatic facility in Iraq, official says; there are no known casualties
item.description: The strike hit the Baghdad Diplomatic Support Center, a sprawling logistical hub for American diplomats near Iraqi military bases and the Baghdad airport, which have come under repeated attacks since the start of the U.S. offensive. A security official said the attack was likely carried out by pro-Tehran militias.

item.title: These are the foods that may help — or harm — your sleep
item.description: Can’t sleep? While there aren’t any magic fixes, these eating patterns, over time, should help.

item.title: Why Congress keeps handing Trump its power
item.description: The president has started a war and levied sweeping tariffs, marking a major expansion of executive power at the expense of the legislative branch.

item.title: Pentagon says about 140 troops injured, 8 severely, in war with Iran
item.description: The number of wounded underscores the pernicious threat of Iranian drones and missiles, which have targeted U.S. bases across the Middle East.

item.title: Whistleblower claims ex-DOGE member says he took Social Security data to his new job
item.description: The Social Security inspector general’s office is investigating allegations that the former DOGE engineer took sensitive data on a thumb drive in a major potential security breach, people familiar said.

item.title: Why are Americans ‘Chinamaxxing’? Look in the mirror.
item.description: The popular social media trend doesn’t mean China is winning the soft power war.

item.title: For-profit colleges, once accused of duping students, hope to rebound under Trump
item.description: A group that represents many of the schools praised the administration’s plans to avoid holding for-profit schools to a higher standard.

item.title: Smartmatic says it’s being targeted by Justice Dept. to bolster false 2020 claims
item.description: The voting machine company asked a judge to dismiss foreign bribery charges filed against it last year, alleging a vindictive and selective prosecution.

item.title: Look who is wrong about the economy now
item.description: Americans called it: This economy is not what it seems.

item.title: Iran war’s oil-price shock fuels GOP anxiety as Trump sends mixed messages
item.description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said Tuesday would be “our most intense day of strikes” as Republicans worried about the political risks of a long conflict.

item.title: Her last letter in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.

item.title: Republican anxiety. Middle East. Zohran Mamdani. Supreme Court. Immigrant arrests. Spring break. Sleepy foods. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Former D.C. police officer accused in multiple sexual assault cases
item.description: Police say Timothy Valentin, 30, met D.C.-area women on dating apps, then drugged and assaulted them.

item.title: Will there be a super El Niño later this year? Here’s what that would mean.
item.description: New data suggests this has the potential to become one of the strongest El Niño events on record.

item.title: Early Iran strikes cost $5.6 billion in munitions, intensifying concerns that U.S. ate into scarce supply of advanced weaponry
item.description: The Pentagon estimate, accounting for the war’s first two days, raises new questions about the Trump administration’s broad dismissal of lawmakers’ concerns that the Iran operation is quickly eroding the military’s readiness.

item.title: FBI obtains voting data from review of 2020 election in Arizona that confirmed Trump’s loss
item.description: The records, obtained via a federal grand jury’s subpoena, represent a new front in the Trump administration’s expanding hunt for evidence that has still never surfaced to support the president’s claims of widespread election fraud. Election officials and Democrats have expressed fears that the administration is building an argument for interfering in this year’s midterm elections.

item.title: Your salted caramel mocha latte is destroying society
item.description: Edmund Burke would have had thoughts about the consumption of that vanilla sweet cream nitro cold brew.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, who revealed Nixon’s White House tapes, dies at 99
item.description: Mr. Butterfield, a onetime aide to Richard M. Nixon, electrified the Watergate investigation with his bombshell testimony about the president’s secret recording system.

item.title: Letter written by Mary, Queen of Scots, before her beheading enthralls crowds centuries later
item.description: The manuscript was a big part of why the 44-year-old royal, who spent half her life imprisoned, didn’t just fade away as a headless footnote. Instead, she continues to command cultlike fascination, revered as a doomed ingenue, a Catholic martyr and a shape-shifting symbol of Scottish identity.

item.title: IndyCar race coming to downtown Washington this summer
item.description: Drivers are expected to zoom past downtown landmarks at speeds as high as 185 mph during the Freedom 250 Grand Prix, part of the nation’s 250th anniversary celebration.

item.title: Video appears to show U.S. Tomahawk missile hit Iranian naval base near elementary school
item.description: The video, which was verified by The Washington Post, is the latest indication of likely U.S. involvement in the attack on Feb. 28 that killed dozens of children in the southern Iranian city of Minab.

item.title: Anthropic sues Pentagon over being labeled a national security risk
item.description: The move comes after President Donald Trump ordered agencies to stop using Anthropic’s AI, amid a bitter feud over how its chatbot Claude might be used in war.

item.title: Live Nation, Ticketmaster agree to settle antitrust lawsuit with Justice Department
item.description: The deal, which must still be approved by a judge, is scheduled to be made public Monday in federal court in Manhattan, said a senior Justice Department official who spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss the terms of the still-undisclosed agreement.

item.title: Global markets slide over oil fears as governments scramble to contain barrel prices
item.description: The Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq composite index and S&P 500 each were down about 1 percent to start the day. Earlier, Japan’s Nikkei index plunged about 5 percent and European stock markets shed 1 to 2 percent. Meanwhile, analysts warned of U.S. pump prices of $4 a gallon, an important psychological barrier that, unlike most economic numbers, is broadcast from street corners nationwide.

item.title: Israeli officials are growing concerned, David Ignatius writes
item.description: A bombing campaign nearing its military goals in Iran leaves the hardest questions unanswered.

item.title: A job shakeup in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Monday points.

item.title: After slashing federal jobs, Trump administration ramps up hiring
item.description: The reversal reflects a retreat from one of the president's early priorities. It could give the White House greater influence over a civilian workforce of 2 million.
